moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Zukertort Opening, one of the most flexible first moves in chess. By developing our knight to f3 before committing our center pawns, we keep maximum flexibility—we can support e4, prepare a kingside fianchetto, or pursue various middlegame plans. The knight immediately controls e5 and d4, two critical central squares, while remaining actively placed.

Black's move is unusual and this is already the "Drunken Cavalry" variation—the name hints at how unconventional this knight placement is! Moving to a6 seems passive since the knight often belongs on c6 or e6, but Black might be trying to avoid standard openings or has ideas to reroute the knight. This move does control b4 and c5, but it places the knight on the board's rim, which generally violates opening principles.

Now we commit to the center, establishing our most important opening goal. With e4 played, we control the critical d5 and f5 squares, and we've opened the diagonal for our light-squared bishop. This move supports our knight on f3 and prepares to build a strong central presence—this is the natural follow-up to Nf3, and it's what most opponents will expect.

Black compounds the earlier oddity by moving the knight again to the edge of the board. The knight on h6 is poorly placed—it aims only at g4 and f5, and it's far from where Black needs piece activity (the queenside and center). This move suggests Black is either attempting an extremely offbeat gambit idea or is making it easy for us to gain a comfortable advantage through superior piece placement.
